\section{Multi-Stakeholder Buying Committees}
\index{Multi-Stakeholder Buying Committees}
Selling to an enterprise is like getting the extended family to agree on a restaurant. Exactly, every department wants a say before signing a cheque. So one enthusiastic contact isn't...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Multi-Stakeholder Buying Committees}

\index{Multi-Stakeholder Buying Committees}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Selling to an enterprise is like getting the extended family to agree on a restaurant. Speaker 2: Exactly, every department wants a say before signing a cheque. Speaker 1: So one enthusiastic contact isn't enough? Speaker 2: Not even close—you need consensus across the organisation.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Multi-Stakeholder Buying Committees

Navigating complex enterprise deals is like choosing a restaurant with the whole family

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Enterprise purchase journey}

\index{Enterprise purchase journey}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: What's the usual path a big company takes before buying? Speaker 2: They gather requirements, issue an RFP and shortlist vendors for demos. Speaker 1: Then comes a pilot or proof of concept before contract talks. Speaker 2: And at a bank, even tiny deals sit in security and legal reviews for months—classic ITIL change control.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Enterprise purchase journey

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Requirements, RFP and vendor shortlist
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pilot or proof of concept to test fit
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Contract negotiation with procurement
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Security and legal reviews before rollout
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Roles in the committee}

\index{Roles in the committee}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Who sits on these committees anyway? Speaker 2: There's an economic buyer with budget, a technical buyer checking architecture, and a champion pushing the project. Speaker 1: Legal, compliance and even end users weigh in too. Speaker 2: Plus IT ops and procurement hunting for red flags—each has a different worry to address.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Roles in the committee

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Economic buyer controls budget
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Technical buyer validates fit
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Champion pushes internally
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Legal/compliance vets obligations
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item End users and IT ops assess practicality
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Procurement checks costs
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Long deal cycles}

\index{Long deal cycles}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: These deals seem to drag on forever. Speaker 2: Six to twelve months is normal once procurement insists on getting the lowest price for the longest time. Speaker 1: What keeps them moving forward? Speaker 2: Regular check-ins and a clear paper trail so momentum isn't lost.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Long deal cycles

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Six to twelve months of meetings
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Pilots, security and compliance reviews
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Procurement stretches pricing talks
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Patience and documentation win
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Mapping consensus}

\index{Mapping consensus}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: How do you win over so many voices? Speaker 2: Map out influencers and tailor messages to their goals. Speaker 1: A hospital project means doctors, IT ops, finance and compliance all get different slides. Speaker 2: Right, when each person feels heard the group finally moves to yes.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Mapping consensus

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Identify influencers and blockers
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Tailor value to each role
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Hospital projects need doctors, IT, finance and compliance aligned
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track decisions over time
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: So closing big deals is more diplomacy than pitching. Speaker 2: Totally. The win comes when every stakeholder believes the solution helps them. Speaker 1: Graduates might be the analyst keeping track of all those voices. Speaker 2: Stick with it—the marathon finish can reshape a vendor's entire year.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Key takeaway

Big deals close when every stakeholder sees their needs addressed—and grads may help herd the cats.

---

\end{quote}


\section{Service Performance Monitoring}
\index{Service Performance Monitoring}
Welcome to our discussion on monitoring vendor performance long after the contract is signed. Many organisations breathe a sigh of relief once a solution goes live and forget to keep...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Service Performance Monitoring}

\index{Service Performance Monitoring}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Welcome to our discussion on monitoring vendor performance long after the contract is signed. Many organisations breathe a sigh of relief once a solution goes live and forget to keep score. Speaker 2: But that's when subtle issues creep in. Maybe support response times stretch out or promised features remain half baked. Without metrics you might not notice until renewal is looming. Speaker 1: We'll explore the key indicators worth tracking and how to turn those numbers into real improvements. Think of it as a health check for your vendor relationships. Speaker 2: Let's dive into why ongoing monitoring matters.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Service Performance Monitoring

Staying proactive after go-live

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Why watch metrics beyond renewals?}

\index{Why watch metrics beyond renewals?}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: After onboarding, vendors sometimes settle into autopilot. Issues get triaged slower and the original enthusiasm fades. Speaker 2: Monitoring gives you evidence of that drift. For example, if average ticket resolution time climbs month over month, you can challenge the vendor before the decline becomes normal. Speaker 1: It's also leverage for negotiations. When renewal time comes, you want hard numbers showing whether service levels were met. Speaker 2: Continuous monitoring keeps both sides honest and ensures you spot trends early rather than scrambling during contract talks.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why watch metrics beyond renewals?

Even after the ink dries on a contract, you need visibility into how well the vendor performs day to day. Service quality can drift over time as priorities change or teams turn over. Monitoring keeps small problems from becoming chronic frustrations. For instance, a help desk might respond quickly during onboarding but grow slower once the vendor has your business locked in. Tracking trends shows whether promised service levels still hold true a year later. Ultimately, performance monitoring gives you data to negotiate improvements rather than relying on vague impressions.

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Service quality KPIs}

\index{Service quality KPIs}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: So what should we measure? Start with the basics: how quickly the vendor responds and resolves support tickets, and how often the service goes down. Speaker 2: Include accuracy metrics too. If releases regularly break integrations, that's a red flag. Speaker 1: Customer satisfaction surveys reveal whether users feel supported. Adoption rates also matter because an unused feature provides no value even if it works perfectly. Speaker 2: Combine these metrics for a holistic view of quality rather than focusing on a single number.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Service quality KPIs

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Response and resolution times for support tickets
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Percentage of uptime and outage duration
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Accuracy of change or release deployments
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Customer satisfaction surveys and Net Promoter Scores
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Adoption or usage rates for key features
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Turning insights into actions}

\index{Turning insights into actions}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Numbers don't mean much unless you act on them. Schedule regular reviews with your vendor to discuss trends. Speaker 2: Bring a short list of the worst metrics and ask what they're doing about them. Maybe a spike in outages points to hardware upgrades or more training. Speaker 1: Assign owners for each action item so it doesn't vanish into thin air. Then check progress at the next meeting. Speaker 2: Over time these small improvements compound into significant service gains, proving the monitoring effort is worthwhile.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Turning insights into actions

Collecting KPIs is just the start. Regularly review the numbers with your vendor and agree on improvement plans. Maybe recurring outages show the need for extra capacity, or survey feedback suggests better documentation. Document the action items, assign owners and follow up next review. An ongoing feedback loop turns raw metrics into tangible service upgrades and helps justify renewal decisions.

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: The main point is simple—measure continuously and act on what you learn. Speaker 2: Keep a dashboard of KPIs handy so you can spot trends early. Share it with your vendor and agree on goals for improvement. Speaker 1: When renewal rolls around, those numbers will guide your negotiation strategy and highlight success stories. Speaker 1: Think of the metrics as your map; without them, you're just guessing where to go next. Speaker 2: Ongoing performance tracking turns a one-time purchase into a living partnership that evolves with your needs.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Key takeaway

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track KPIs continuously, not just at renewal time
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Review results jointly and set improvement targets
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use data trends to drive conversations on service quality
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}


\section{Product Team Alignment}
\index{Product Team Alignment}
Ever seen a sales rep promise a feature that doesn't exist yet? Too many times. We call it "selling the roadmap's roadmap." Without product in the room, those promises become expensive...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Product Team Alignment}

\index{Product Team Alignment}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Ever seen a sales rep promise a feature that doesn't exist yet? Speaker 2: Too many times. We call it "selling the roadmap's roadmap." Without product in the room, those promises become expensive IOUs. Speaker 1: So we bring product teams into deals from day one? Speaker 2: Exactly, so the solution matches what's actually on the roadmap.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Product Team Alignment

Selling what the product can truly deliver

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Why involve product early?}

\index{Why involve product early?}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: What's the upside of looping product in early? Speaker 2: We avoid custom promises that engineering can't deliver. Speaker 1: And product hears fresh customer pain points straight from the source. Speaker 2: Which can reshape the roadmap faster than any survey. I've seen customer requests move features up by quarters when product really understands the business impact.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why involve product early?

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Prevents overselling features that don't exist. Early partnership sets realistic expectations so customers aren't building plans around vaporware. It saves product from scrambling to build last-minute features and keeps credibility intact when contracts are signed.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Shapes the roadmap with real customer pain points. Feedback from discovery calls exposes patterns surveys miss; if four prospects all request the same integration, product can re-order sprints to capture revenue sooner.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Builds trust between sales, product and the client. When all three groups share context, engineers feel ownership of deals and customers see a unified front, which shortens cycles and reduces last-minute surprises.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Technical validation steps}

\index{Technical validation steps}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: How do we prove the solution actually works? Speaker 2: Start with a quick architecture review and demo. Speaker 1: Maybe even a mini proof of concept to surface integration issues. Speaker 2: Then confirm timelines so sales isn't selling vaporware. Last month we caught a SSO integration that would have added eight weeks to delivery.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Technical validation steps

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Review architectures and integration points. A quick whiteboard session surfaces mismatched assumptions early, like missing auth flows or incompatible data formats.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Run lightweight demos or proofs of concept. A stripped-down test uncovers performance limits or security gaps before contracts are signed, saving weeks of rework and exposing configuration quirks documentation glosses over.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Confirm timelines for upcoming features so sales isn't selling vaporware. Agree on release dates, backlog owners, what happens if the timeline slips, and document it. This transparency prevents awkward renegotiations later and shows respect for engineering bandwidth.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Example: Customer needs to sync 10,000 user records daily. Product confirms the API handles 5,000/hour max, so sales adjusts the timeline or proposes a phased rollout instead of promising immediate full capacity.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Working rhythm}

\index{Working rhythm}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: What keeps sales and product on the same page? Speaker 2: Weekly syncs and a shared demo environment help. Nothing keeps everyone honest like a demo that breaks mid-presentation. Speaker 1: And when trials end, we feed learnings back to product. Speaker 2: That loop is gold for refining features and messaging.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Working rhythm

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Hold regular syncs between sales engineers and product leads. A 30-minute weekly meeting keeps the pipeline visible and flags product roadblocks before they derail deals.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Share feedback from trials and customer calls. Notes from pilots go straight into backlog grooming so lessons aren't lost and features ship with real market context. Those insights often inspire new features or highlight gaps competitors exploit.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use shared demo environments to test solutions. Maintaining a common sandbox means both teams know exactly what a prospect will see. Nothing keeps everyone honest like a demo that breaks mid-presentation.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Example: Weekly "Sales-Product Sync" — Sales shares top 3 customer objections, Product updates on upcoming releases, and both review the demo environment for the next big pitch.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: When alignment goes wrong}

\index{When alignment goes wrong}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: So aligning early keeps us honest? Speaker 2: Yep. Customers get workable solutions, not promises. Speaker 1: And it opens roles like sales engineer or product manager for tech-savvy grads. Speaker 2: Exactly—cross-functional experience is gold. You learn customer needs and technical limits, making you invaluable to growing companies.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

When alignment goes wrong

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Customer expects feature X, but engineering can't deliver for 6 months. Contract renegotiations and awkward apology tours follow while trust erodes.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sales promises an integration that requires major architecture changes. Developers pull nights and weekends to retrofit systems, and the promised shortcut becomes a maintenance liability long after the deal closes.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Product team learns about a critical use case after the solution is built. Rework erases previous sprint gains and demoralizes the team that had already moved on.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Result: frustrated customers, overworked engineers and missed revenue targets. One misaligned deal can ripple through the roadmap for quarters, crowding out innovation with fire drills.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: How teams stay connected}

\index{How teams stay connected}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: What happens when sales and product fall out of sync? Speaker 2: Customers expect feature X and get told it'll arrive next quarter. Speaker 1: Then engineering pulls all-nighters to patch things together. Speaker 2: Exactly. One misaligned deal can burn trust and derail the roadmap.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

How teams stay connected

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Shared Slack channels for real-time updates. Quick questions about feasibility or bugs get answered in minutes instead of clogging email threads.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Product requirement documents (PRDs) linked to sales opportunities. Everyone references the same source of truth, keeping scope clear as deals evolve.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Customer feedback loops from support to product. Tickets tagged by feature feed directly into prioritization so recurring issues are visible to engineers.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Joint customer calls for complex technical discussions. Seeing engineers and sales strategize together reassures buyers that promises are grounded in reality.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item These channels create a transparent conversation that keeps momentum even when teams are remote or spread across time zones.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: How do teams stay connected day to day? Speaker 2: Shared Slack channels keep questions moving. Speaker 1: Linking PRDs to opportunities keeps scope clear. Speaker 2: And joint customer calls ensure no one makes promises in a vacuum.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Key takeaway

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Early collaboration turns promises into deliverable solutions and opens paths into roles like Sales Engineer or Product Manager. It also keeps engineering from becoming the last-minute rescue squad when sales over-commits.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item For new graduates, cross-functional experience is gold. You learn customer needs, technical constraints, and how strategy becomes shipped features.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Companies look for hires who can bridge conversations; aligning with product shows you can speak both languages and advocate for realistic scopes.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Practice this habit in internships or student projects and you'll be trusted to lead bigger deals sooner, whatever title you carry.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item That blend of credibility and empathy makes you invaluable to growing companies navigating complex customer demands.
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}


\section{Proof-of-Concept Management}
\index{Proof-of-Concept Management}
How many times have you heard "it seemed great in the demo"? Jumping straight into production with a new tool can be risky. That's why teams run a proof of concept—to test the waters...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Proof-of-Concept Management}

\index{Proof-of-Concept Management}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: How many times have you heard "it seemed great in the demo"? Speaker 2: Jumping straight into production with a new tool can be risky. Speaker 1: That's why teams run a proof of concept—to test the waters before committing budget and resources. Speaker 2: The goal is to validate that the vendor can solve the problem and fit into existing workflows. Speaker 1: We'll walk through setting success criteria, keeping scope tight and evaluating results so POCs lead to clear decisions.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Proof-of-Concept Management

Testing ideas before full commitment

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Why run a proof of concept?}

\index{Why run a proof of concept?}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Proofs of concept drift when success isn't defined. Speaker 2: Agree on measurable outcomes—page load times under three seconds or an 80\% user satisfaction score. Speaker 1: Set a short checklist of must-haves and nice-to-haves. Speaker 2: Document the baseline so you can compare results after the trial.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why run a proof of concept?

Running a small trial reduces risk before signing a long contract. Teams can validate functionality, integration and user fit without a full deployment. A short experiment uncovers deal-breakers early and builds evidence to support or reject the vendor.

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Because "it works on the vendor's laptop" isn't a business case
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Define success upfront}

\index{Define success upfront}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Ever seen a two-week POC turn into a three-month mini-project? Speaker 2: Also known as "POC creep"—feature creep's expensive cousin. Speaker 1: Keep the scope tight with limited users, datasets and integrations. Speaker 2: Assign a project lead, vendor contact and schedule regular check-ins. Speaker 1: Set a modest budget and communicate progress to stakeholders so the trial doesn't sprawl.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Define success upfront

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Agree on measurable outcomes like page load times under three seconds or an 80\% user satisfaction score.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Capture a baseline so improvements are clear.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Distinguish must-have criteria from nice-to-have bonuses.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Manage scope and timeline}

\index{Manage scope and timeline}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: When the trial ends, gather metrics and user feedback. Speaker 2: Compare the results against the success criteria—cost per user, integration complexity and training needs. Speaker 1: Decide whether to proceed, adjust or walk away. Speaker 2: Don't let the vendor drag things out hoping you'll forget the initial criteria. Speaker 1: Summarise the findings in a short report so stakeholders understand the recommendation.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Manage scope and timeline

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Limit participants, datasets and integrations.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Example: a marketing team tests a CRM with 10 users and 100 sample leads for two weeks.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Assign a project lead, vendor contact and schedule regular check-ins.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Set a modest budget and update stakeholders regularly to avoid POC creep.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Evaluate and document outcomes}

\index{Evaluate and document outcomes}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Common pitfalls? Free trials that aren't really free. Speaker 2: Or testing with data that's nothing like real life. Speaker 1: Keep an eye out for POC creep and rein in vendors who keep adding "just one more feature." Speaker 2: Avoid these traps and you'll get a clear read on the solution's value.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Evaluate and document outcomes

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Consider evaluation criteria such as cost per user, integration complexity and training requirements.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Gather metrics and feedback at the end of the trial.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Keep the vendor on schedule—don't let them drag out the trial hoping you'll forget the initial criteria.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Decide on go, no-go or adjust and rerun. Share a brief report so leadership understands the recommendation and any follow-up work.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Common POC mistakes}

\index{Common POC mistakes}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: To justify a POC, track what it costs. Speaker 2: Count staff time, licenses and infrastructure. Speaker 1: Then compare those costs to potential savings or revenue. Speaker 2: Share the math so stakeholders see whether the trial paid off.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Common POC mistakes

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Free trials that aren't really free.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Testing with unrealistic or cherry-picked data.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Letting POC creep turn into a mini project.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: ROI calculation}

\index{ROI calculation}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Sometimes the best decision is to cut the trial short. Speaker 2: Define exit criteria upfront so you know when to pull the plug. Speaker 1: Communicate the decision quickly to stakeholders and the vendor. Speaker 2: Capture lessons learned and move on.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

ROI calculation

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Track staff time, licensing and infrastructure costs.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Compare against potential savings or revenue gains.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document assumptions so stakeholders see the math.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Exit strategy}

\index{Exit strategy}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: A good proof of concept either saves you money or makes you money. Speaker 2: A bad one does both—for the vendor. Speaker 1: Define success, control scope and evaluate honestly. Speaker 2: Then you can move forward with evidence or save money by walking away. Speaker 1: Either outcome beats committing blindly.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Exit strategy

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Define criteria to end the trial early if it fails.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Communicate the decision and lessons learned to stakeholders and the vendor.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Key takeaway

A good POC either saves you money or makes you money. A bad POC does both—for the vendor. Disciplined POC management sets expectations, controls risk and provides evidence for investment decisions.

---

\end{quote}


\section{Vendor Risk Management}
\index{Vendor Risk Management}
Vendors make bold promises, but what happens when they change pricing or disappear entirely? If your whole service depends on them, even a minor hiccup can snowball into a major...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Vendor Risk Management}

\index{Vendor Risk Management}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Vendors make bold promises, but what happens when they change pricing or disappear entirely? If your whole service depends on them, even a minor hiccup can snowball into a major disruption. Speaker 2: Exactly. Risk management is about preparing for those "what if" moments before they happen so you're not left scrambling. We'll break down vendor lock-in, data security, business continuity and a few other gotchas that often get overlooked. Speaker 1: Think of it as disaster planning for external relationships. The more you understand potential pitfalls, the easier it is to negotiate contracts and set expectations from the start. Ready to dig in? Speaker 2: Let's do it.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Vendor Risk Management

Protecting continuity and security

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Why worry about vendor lock-in?}

\index{Why worry about vendor lock-in?}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: "Vendor lock-in" means it's painfully difficult to move your data or workflow somewhere else. Think about a car that only runs on fuel from one specific gas station chain. Speaker 2: The longer you rely on their proprietary format, the more trapped you become. I've seen companies spend six months and \$50,000 just to migrate their customer databases to a new CRM. Speaker 1: That's why you negotiate export capabilities and reasonable notice periods up front. Ask to see an export demo during the sales process, not after you've signed a contract. Speaker 2: When switching is possible before you need to switch, you keep the power in the relationship instead of the vendor.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Why worry about vendor lock-in?

Vendor lock-in happens when switching away from a service becomes expensive or

technically difficult. Imagine all your files saved in a format that only one

vendor's software understands. Changing providers might require costly data

conversions or lengthy downtime, so the vendor holds all the power. Real-world

examples range from custom Salesforce integrations that won't export cleanly to

cloud platforms with no easy migration path. Warning signs include contracts

with 12-month notice periods, proprietary data formats, or ongoing integration

fees that grow over time. It's like dating someone who slowly moves all your

stuff to their place—leaving suddenly becomes a major project. Always ask,

"How do we get our data out if we need to leave?" up front and negotiate

reasonable exit clauses before signing anything.

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Safeguarding data security}

\index{Safeguarding data security}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: When you hand over data to a vendor, it's like giving someone the keys to your house. Speaker 2: Right, and it's like a friend borrowing your car—you want to know they have insurance and won't lend it to their teenager. Ask about encryption both at rest and in transit. Speaker 1: Don't forget access controls. If anyone at the vendor can peek at your information, that's a problem. Speaker 2: A mature vendor will show you compliance certificates and walk you through their incident response plan. If they get hacked, how quickly will they tell you? Speaker 1: And check their history. A breach isn't always a deal breaker, but silence or slow notifications definitely are.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Safeguarding data security

Handing over data to a vendor is like giving someone the keys to your house.

You want to know their locks are strong and they actually monitor who comes and

goes. Ask about encryption both in transit and at rest, and check whether staff

undergo background checks before they can access your information. Find out

where servers are located because privacy laws differ around the world. Don't be

shy about their breach history—have they ever disclosed incidents and how long

did notification take? A reputable vendor will gladly provide documentation such

as SOC 2 or ISO 27001 certifications and will outline their incident response

process. If they dodge questions or claim "trust us," that's a red flag. Asking

about security shouldn't feel like interrogating a spy; if they're evasive,

that's your answer right there.

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Business continuity planning}

\index{Business continuity planning}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Business continuity is your lifeboat when a vendor hits rough seas. Speaker 2: Backup plans are like fire drills—they only work if you've actually practiced them. Ask for proof that their backups actually work—a written plan is meaningless if they've never restored from it. Speaker 1: Some companies schedule practice runs where they simulate a complete outage and measure how quickly systems come back online. That's the kind of evidence you want. Speaker 2: Also think about dependencies. If your vendor goes down, do you have a secondary provider, or at least an exit clause that lets you bring operations in-house? Speaker 1: Having these options ahead of time turns a potential disaster into a temporary inconvenience.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Business continuity planning

Every vendor will claim they have backups, but have those backups ever been

restored in a real emergency? Ask to see test results or drills where the vendor

simulated a failure and recovered systems. Business continuity also means

thinking beyond a single provider. Maintain a secondary supplier or at least a

clear exit clause so you can pivot if their service goes dark. Map out

escalation paths for different severities—who do you call if the service is down

for an hour versus a day? Having a well-practiced plan keeps you operating when

surprises strike, just like carrying an umbrella even when the forecast looks

sunny.

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Financial risk assessment}

\index{Financial risk assessment}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: A well-crafted risk plan keeps surprises from derailing your overall strategy. Speaker 2: Exactly. When you've covered lock-in, security, continuity and finances, a vendor hiccup becomes an inconvenience instead of a crisis. Speaker 1: The time you invest early on saves countless hours later—and often a lot of money. Speaker 2: Keep reviewing your vendors annually. Markets shift, products evolve and new regulations pop up, so a "set and forget" approach won't cut it. Speaker 1: Staying proactive is the best insurance policy for long-term success.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Financial risk assessment

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Analyse licensing models carefully: is pricing based on users, data volume or something else entirely?
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Watch for per-seat pricing that looks cheap at 10 users but explodes to \$50K at 500.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Factor in currency swings and automatic renewal clauses that increase fees annually.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Weigh the stability of startups versus established firms—one may cost less but carry higher risk.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Case study: one firm spent \$200K migrating from Oracle to PostgreSQL after licensing fees spiked.
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Thorough financial reviews early on prevent nasty surprises later.
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Operational dependencies}

\index{Operational dependencies}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Hidden costs are often the biggest surprise with a new vendor. Speaker 2: So what should we look out for in pricing structures? Licensing tiers, data overage charges and currency swings can blow the budget fast. Speaker 1: I've seen startups lure customers with low introductory pricing, then double rates after a year when you're deeply integrated. Per-seat licensing that seems cheap at ten users may balloon to fifty thousand dollars at five hundred. Speaker 2: That's why you model best- and worst-case scenarios before signing. Ask how renewals are calculated and whether discounts vanish if usage dips. Speaker 1: A solid financial assessment now prevents nasty surprises later and helps you decide whether a cheaper vendor actually costs more in the long run.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Operational dependencies

Relying on a single vendor can create a fragile chain of dependencies. If their service goes down, do your internal processes grind to a halt? Consider how long it would take staff to learn a replacement system or rebuild integrations. A real-world outage, like Slack's six-hour downtime in 2021, left many teams scrambling because chatbots and help desks all flowed through a single tool. Document alternative workflows—when Slack is down, teams might revert to email threads and phone trees—and build training time into your budget so switching providers doesn't paralyse your organisation.

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Legal and compliance considerations}

\index{Legal and compliance considerations}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Operational risk creeps in when one vendor becomes the single point of failure for your processes. Speaker 2: Think about how many teams rely on Slack or Teams for day-to-day work. When the service went down for hours in 2021, many companies had no backup communication plan. Speaker 1: Documenting alternative workflows and training staff on them keeps you from grinding to a halt if a tool goes offline. Speaker 2: Budget time for switching, too. If a new vendor requires retraining or rewriting integrations, include that effort in your planning. Speaker 1: Reducing dependency is about being ready to pivot before you're forced to.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Legal and compliance considerations

Data sovereignty laws and industry regulations can dictate where and how vendor services operate. Ensure that contracts spell out who owns the data, how it may be used and what liability the vendor carries if they breach privacy or security requirements. Ask about compliance certifications relevant to your sector. For example, healthcare providers must verify HIPAA compliance before putting records in the cloud, while retailers may need PCI DSS for payment details. Some jurisdictions require your data to remain in-country, so confirm server locations and backup storage. Insurance coverage and indemnity clauses also play a role; they define who pays if things go wrong. Understanding these legal obligations up front reduces headaches later.

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Due diligence checklist}

\index{Due diligence checklist}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Legal risks often hide in the fine print of vendor agreements. Speaker 2: Data residency clauses, privacy regulations and industry-specific laws can limit where your information is stored or processed. Speaker 1: Ask vendors which compliance certifications they hold and whether they've ever failed an audit. Speaker 2: Also clarify liability. If they mishandle your data or violate regulations, who pays the fines or remediation costs? Speaker 1: Compliance fatigue is real for small businesses, but skipping due diligence leaves you exposed. Speaker 2: To wrap up, confirm data location, certification status and liability provisions before you sign anything.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Due diligence checklist

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Evaluate financial stability and customer references
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Review security certifications and audit reports
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Test data export options before signing
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Red flags during selection}

\index{Red flags during selection}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Red flags during selection

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Evasive answers about pricing or compliance
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item No references or vague case studies
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Unrealistic implementation timelines
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 10: Exit strategy planning}

\index{Exit strategy planning}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Exit strategy planning

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Negotiate clear termination clauses and notice periods
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document data migration steps and associated costs
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Keep alternative vendors in mind in case you need to switch
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 11: Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

\paragraph{Narration.} \emph{No narration text is currently available for this slide.}

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Key takeaway

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Ask about data export options before signing
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Verify security certifications and test backups
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Schedule regular reviews to keep risks visible
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document who owns each vendor relationship internally
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Hold a quarterly risk review to track emerging issues
\end{itemize}

---

\end{quote}


\section{Sales Engineering Support}
\index{Sales Engineering Support}
Ever wonder who makes the slick demo look like magic? That's the sales engineer. They're part coder, part translator, and part stagehand with a laptop. Most start as curious developers or...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Sales Engineering Support}

\index{Sales Engineering Support}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Ever wonder who makes the slick demo look like magic? That's the sales engineer. Speaker 2: They're part coder, part translator, and part stagehand with a laptop. Speaker 1: Most start as curious developers or support interns and stumble into helping sales. Speaker 2: Suddenly they're explaining APIs to executives and calming nervous product managers. Speaker 1: The role mixes technical breadth with people skills and a dash of improvisation. Speaker 2: If that sounds like your lab group presentations, you're already halfway there. Speaker 1: Let's explore how sales engineers steer deals from first pitch to final handoff. Speaker 2: And maybe share a few war stories along the way.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Sales Engineering Support

Turning ideas into workable solutions

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Demo environments}

\index{Demo environments}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: First stop is the demo environment, the stage set for prospects. Speaker 2: We clone real systems, scrub the data, and swap "John Doe" for something believable. Speaker 1: Like spinning up a CRM with actual regional sales numbers so dashboards don't scream Lorem ipsum. Speaker 2: We script scenarios based on discovery calls and keep snapshots so the next reset takes minutes. Speaker 1: A clean sandbox lets stakeholders click around without fear of breaking production. Speaker 2: When they see their world reflected, the product stops being abstract and starts feeling inevitable.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Demo environments

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Build realistic sandboxes with sanitized customer-style data
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Script scenarios from discovery calls for believable workflows
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Maintain snapshots so resets take minutes
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Because nobody wants to see "Lorem ipsum" in a mission-critical dashboard
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Proof of concept vs demo}

\index{Proof of concept vs demo}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Sometimes a slide and sandbox won't cut it, and a proof of concept enters the chat. Speaker 2: Demos are show-and-tell; POCs are science labs with success criteria, deadlines, and occasionally panic. Speaker 1: We wire our tool into a subset of real systems, maybe pushing data through an API or syncing single sign-on. Speaker 2: If it fails, we fix it fast or gracefully bow out before everyone wastes more coffee. Speaker 1: A well-scoped POC can win a deal; a vague one eats weeks. Speaker 2: So we time-box, document results, and celebrate when the test data finally flows.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Proof of concept vs demo

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Demos show; POCs prove integration under real conditions
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Time-box POCs with clear success criteria
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Run POCs when stakes are high or workflows are unique
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Celebrate when the test data finally flows
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: RFP responses}

\index{RFP responses}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Then comes the request for proposal, a hundred pages of "Can it do X?" disguised as bedtime reading. Speaker 2: Sales engineers become translators, turning those questions into answers product, security, and legal can sign. Speaker 1: We tackle sections on technical requirements, security compliance, and integration timelines, flagging every "it depends". Speaker 2: Competitive evaluations mean highlighting differentiators without promising unicorns. Speaker 1: Common pitfalls? Forgetting to note a limitation or mismatching version numbers. Speaker 2: We keep a library of past responses and diagrams to save time and sanity. Speaker 1: Clear assumptions build trust and move us to the short list. Speaker 2: And yes, coffee is required.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

RFP responses

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Address sections like technical requirements, security compliance, integration timelines
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Translate "Can it do X?" into qualified answers with diagrams and caveats
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Coordinate with product, security, and legal teams
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Highlight differentiators without promising unicorns
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Handling technical objections}

\index{Handling technical objections}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: No deal survives first contact with the prospect's skeptical engineer. Speaker 2: They ask about latency, failover, and whether our API speaks their quirky legacy protocol. Speaker 1: We prep a FAQ of common objections and a stash of whiteboard markers for architecture doodles. Speaker 2: Admitting limitations beats bluffing; offering workarounds keeps the conversation alive. Speaker 1: When someone says "It should just work," that's our cue to grab more coffee. Speaker 2: Handling objections calmly shows credibility and often uncovers hidden requirements. Speaker 1: Win or lose, the tech debate sharpens the product for the next call.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Handling technical objections

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Prepare FAQ and architecture sketches for common blockers
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Use whiteboards or diagramming tools to explain designs
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Admit limitations and offer workarounds
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item When someone says "It should just work," brew more coffee
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 6: Integration planning}

\index{Integration planning}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Integration planning happens before ink hits the contract. Speaker 2: We send discovery questionnaires and hop on technical interviews to map the landscape. Speaker 1: Are we talking SSO through SAML, nightly data migration, or a firehose of API events? Speaker 2: We diagram data flows, flag custom work, and rate each dependency for risk. Speaker 1: Catching a missing OAuth scope now beats a meltdown after launch. Speaker 2: These plans become the blueprint for delivery teams and save everyone from scope-creep déjà vu. Speaker 1: Integration isn't magic; it's careful choreography. Speaker 2: And yes, there will be surprises—so we plan for those too.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Integration planning

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Send discovery questionnaires and run technical interviews
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Map SSO, API, and data migration paths early
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Assess dependencies and risk before contracts are signed
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Provide blueprints that prevent scope creep later
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 7: Post-sale handoff}

\index{Post-sale handoff}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Once the deal closes, we don't just drop the mic and vanish. Speaker 2: A smooth handoff to implementation is the difference between happy clients and angry emails. Speaker 1: We package demo configs, POC notes, and every "it depends" conversation. Speaker 2: Kickoff calls align expectations and introduce the delivery team who actually get weekends off. Speaker 1: We stick around for early milestones, translating any last-minute surprises. Speaker 2: Good documentation prevents the classic "what was promised" debate three months later. Speaker 1: Then we reload our sandbox for the next adventure.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Post-sale handoff

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Share demo and POC context with implementation teams
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Schedule kickoff meetings and knowledge transfer sessions
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Document decisions so "what was promised" stays clear
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 8: Tools of the trade}

\index{Tools of the trade}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Every sales engineer has a toolkit that rivals Batman's belt. Speaker 2: Sandbox environments, feature flags, and data generators keep demos from combusting. Speaker 1: Diagramming tools like Lucidchart or the back of a napkin explain architectures in seconds. Speaker 2: We live in ticketing systems, version control, and chat apps where GIFs are a second language. Speaker 1: A library of scripts automates resets when the demo goes sideways. Speaker 2: And yes, caffeine counts as a tool—espresso for outages, tea for security reviews. Speaker 1: The right toolkit turns panic into polish.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Tools of the trade

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sandbox environments, feature flags, and data generators
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Diagramming tools like Lucidchart or Visio
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Collaboration suites and ticketing systems
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Caffeine in many forms
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 9: Career snapshot}

\index{Career snapshot}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: So how do you get this gig if you're still in school? Speaker 2: Many sales engineers start as interns, support analysts, or hackers who can also talk to humans. Speaker 1: You need broad technical curiosity, decent coding chops, and the ability to explain jargon to your aunt. Speaker 2: A typical day mixes discovery calls, lab tinkering, and the occasional emergency demo repair. Speaker 1: From here you can jump into product management, solutions architecture, or lead a team. Speaker 2: It's a career for people who like variety and don't mind living in both PowerPoint and terminal windows.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Career snapshot

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Entry paths: internships, support roles, or curious developers
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Skills: technical breadth, communication, and improvisation
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Typical day: discovery calls, lab tinkering, and demos
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Growth: product management, solutions architecture, or team leadership
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 10: Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Sales engineers are the bridge from promise to production. Speaker 2: We craft demos, wrangle POCs, fend off objections, and sketch integration plans on every available surface. Speaker 1: Then we hand the blueprint to delivery teams without dropping context. Speaker 2: The role rewards curiosity, patience, and a willingness to reboot a server five minutes before showtime. Speaker 1: For students, it's a path that lets you stay technical while shaping how real products land with customers. Speaker 2: And if you like turning "it should just work" into "it works," you'll fit right in.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Key takeaway

Sales engineers bridge vision and delivery by proving capability, shaping proposals, handling objections, and preparing implementation paths.

---

\end{quote}


\section{Salesforce Opportunity Walkthrough}
\index{Salesforce Opportunity Walkthrough}
In this walkthrough we're putting ourselves in the shoes of a Salesforce account executive taking a deal from first t ouch to signature. Instead of abstract theory, we'll follow a named...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Salesforce Opportunity Walkthrough}

\index{Salesforce Opportunity Walkthrough}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: In this walkthrough we're putting ourselves in the shoes of a Salesforce account executive taking a deal from first t ouch to signature. Speaker 2: Instead of abstract theory, we'll follow a named customer, a real pipeline flow, and highlight the collaboration poin ts with solution engineering and service transition.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Salesforce Opportunity Walkthrough

From curious lead to signed deal

Learning objectives

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Follow a practical Salesforce flow for progressing a B2B tech opportunity
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Understand which fields and artifacts matter at each stage
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item See where sales, solution and service teams collaborate before close
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Scenario setup}

\index{Scenario setup}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Our company is CloudOps Solutions and the prospect is RiverBank, a regulated financial services firm. Speaker 2: That context matters. It means longer security reviews, change freezes around quarter-end, and a bigger cast of stake holders. We'll use Salesforce Lightning to keep the moving pieces under control.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Scenario setup

We play the role of an Account Executive at CloudOps Solutions, selling a managed DevOps platform. Our prospect is RiverBank, a

regional financial services firm modernising its release process. We'll use Salesforce Lightning to capture data, automate task

s and coordinate with pre-sales and delivery teams.

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Step 1 – Capture the lead}

\index{Step 1 – Capture the lead}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: The journey starts with a marketing-generated lead. Priya Shah attended our DevOps compliance webinar. Speaker 2: We import her details, tag the industry, and create a follow-up task. Pausing the nurture emails prevents duplicate me ssaging while sales takes over.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Step 1 – Capture the lead

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Marketing imports RiverBank CTO Priya Shah from a webinar list
\end{itemize}

\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Auto-assigned to the enterprise sales queue with nurture emails paused
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Initial task: call within 24 hours to confirm interest and challenges
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Step 2 – Qualify and convert}

\index{Step 2 – Qualify and convert}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: During the first call we confirm BANT—budget, authority, need, and timeline. Priya wants to cut incident-related change failures in half before year-end. Speaker 2: Once qualified, we convert the lead. Salesforce creates the account, contact and opportunity records in one move. We also assign our solution engineer Sarah—she speaks fluent compliance—and our customer success partner Mike, who has yet to meet a regulatory framework he couldn't charm, so discovery is collaborative from day one.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Step 2 – Qualify and convert

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Discovery call confirms budget, authority, need and timeline (BANT)
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]

\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Convert lead → create Account "RiverBank", Contact "Priya Shah", and Opportunity "RiverBank – DevOps Platform"
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]

\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

Speaker 1: In discovery we document the current release process, incident history and compliance guardrails. Speaker 2: Salesforce Path guidance nudges us to attach agendas, meeting notes and stakeholder maps. Priya mentions three production incidents last quarter during code releases, so we log that in the Pain Points field and flag it for our solution engineer to tackle in the technical demo. The shared notes doc keeps sales, engineering and service transition aligned on what we learn.

Finance is alerted for invoicing, customer success gets the onboarding checklist, and we document the mutual action plan so everyone knows the next steps.

\section{Tech Sales vs Other Industries}
\index{Tech Sales vs Other Industries}
Selling software isn't like handing over car keys and waving goodbye. Right, no more "always be closing"—now it's "always be retaining". The relationship keeps going as long as customers...

\subsection*{Slide 1: Tech Sales vs Other Industries}

\index{Tech Sales vs Other Industries}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Selling software isn't like handing over car keys and waving goodbye. Speaker 2: Right, no more "always be closing"—now it's "always be retaining". Speaker 1: The relationship keeps going as long as customers log in. Speaker 2: Exactly, upgrades and renewals keep both sides talking.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Tech Sales vs Other Industries

Recurring revenue and rapid change

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 2: Recurring revenue models}

\index{Recurring revenue models}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Recurring revenue sounds great but doesn't it add pressure to retain every client? Speaker 2: Totally. Netflix's shift from DVDs to streaming showed how one lost viewer hurts the model. Speaker 1: So support teams hover after sign-off. Speaker 2: They need proof people use the product or the subscription won't renew.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Recurring revenue models

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Subscriptions replace one-off deals
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Retention is king—no more "always be closing"
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Netflix's DVD-to-streaming shift shows subscription power
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Free trials hook users before the first invoice
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 3: Land and expand mindset}

\index{Land and expand mindset}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: What's this land-and-expand idea everyone mentions? Speaker 2: You win a small deal first, show value, then grow the footprint inside that account. Speaker 1: Salesforce started with a few sales reps before taking over whole companies. Speaker 2: Slack and Zoom even seed free teams; sales engineers jump in once usage explodes.

\paragraph{On-screen material.}

\begin{quote}\small\raggedright

Land and expand mindset

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Start small then grow account value
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Salesforce landed a team then rolled out company-wide
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Slack and Zoom seed free teams before sales calls
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sales engineers help validate technical fit
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 4: Rapid product cycles}

\index{Rapid product cycles}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Features seem to change every sprint. How do sales teams keep up? Speaker 2: Weekly briefings with product and sales engineers keep pitches current. Speaker 1: Then they watch dashboards like parents tracking a teenager to see who tries the new stuff. Speaker 2: Adoption cues them on which upgrade to mention next.

Rapid product cycles

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]

\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Reps and sales engineers brief constantly
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Dashboards show which features customers adopt
\end{itemize}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Product-led growth turns usage into new pitches
\end{itemize}

\end{quote}

\subsection*{Slide 5: Metrics obsession}

\index{Metrics obsession}

\paragraph{Narration.}

Speaker 1: Why do tech companies obsess over ARR and churn rates? Speaker 2: Those numbers predict the health of the business better than one-off bookings. Speaker 1: Freemium funnels must convert or churn wipes out growth. Speaker 2: Exactly, reps stare at usage dashboards like hawks.

Metrics obsession
